---
summary: 'Writing action handlers — the write side. Includes the change-stream contract and transactions.'
title: 'Actions'
---

# Actions

An action is a **write tool** your app exposes — the kind of tool meant to mutate `data.sqlite`. The tool definition lives in `app.json` → `actions[]`; this page is about writing the **implementation** in `actions/<name>.js`. Callers reach the tool through the generic `centraid_write` dispatcher. See [Queries and actions](/concepts/queries-and-actions) for the framing.

After an action returns successfully, the runtime emits a `change` event on `/centraid/<id>/_changes` listing the tables your SQL actually touched — that is how subscribed iframes learn to re-fetch. (See [the change-stream contract](#the-change-stream-contract) below for how the table list is determined — it is _not_ read from `writes[]`.)

```js
// actions/set-cups.js
export default async ({ body, db }) => {
  const next = Math.max(0, Math.min(8, Number(body?.cups ?? 0)));
  const today = todayKey();
  await db
    .prepare(
      `INSERT INTO hydrate_daily (date, cups) VALUES (?, ?)
       ON CONFLICT(date) DO UPDATE SET cups = excluded.cups`,
    )
    .run(today, next);
  return { status: 200, body: { date: today, cups: next, goal: 8 } };
};
```

## The handler argument

An action handler is the **default export** — a single async function taking one destructured object:

| Field    | Type        | What                                                                           |
| -------- | ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `body`   | object      | The caller's input, already validated against `actions[].input` in `app.json`. |
| `params` | object      | Reserved (currently always `{}`).                                              |
| `db`     | `ScopedDb`  | The app's `data.sqlite`, via the async proxy.                                  |
| `log`    | `ScopedLog` | `log.info` / `log.warn` / `log.error`.                                         |
| `app`    | `AppRef`    | `{ id, dir }`.                                                                 |
| `ctx`    | object      | `{ fetch, abortSignal }` — a timeout-bound `fetch` and its abort signal.       |

The validated input arrives as `body` (queries call the same value `input`/`query` — only the name differs). It is already Ajv-validated against the manifest schema, so you don't re-validate.

```js
export default async ({ body, db }) => {
  const cups = body.cups; // safe to use directly — already schema-checked
  // …
};
```

The `db` proxy and `ctx` surface are identical to queries — every `db.prepare(...).run/.get/.all` and `db.exec` is async and must be awaited; `ctx` is only `fetch` + `abortSignal` (no `fs`, no `process`). See [Queries → the async db proxy](/build/queries#the-async-db-proxy).

## The return shape

An action may return its payload **directly**, or wrap it in the legacy envelope:

```ts
{ status?: number; body?: unknown }
```

- If you return `{ status, body }`, the dispatcher **unwraps `body`** and hands that to the caller. A `status >= 400` is converted into a `HANDLER_ERROR` (so an error JSON isn't silently passed off as success).
- If you return a plain value (no `status`), it is passed through as-is.

Both forms are accepted; new handlers can skip the envelope. If you **throw**, the dispatcher catches it and returns a `HANDLER_ERROR` envelope (HTTP 500 via the shim) — don't try/catch just to swallow.

## The change-stream contract

This is the part most worth getting right. `writes: []` in the manifest is **documentation** — it lists the tables you intend to mutate, for chat permissions and readers. **It does not drive change events.**

What actually fires the stream: the runtime wraps each action handler in a SQLite **session** and, after the handler returns successfully, decodes which tables the session's changeset touched. It emits one `change` event with that observed, deduplicated, sorted table list. So:

- If your handler writes a table you forgot to list in `writes`, the stream **still** fires for it (the observation is from real SQL, not the declaration).
- If a handler makes no writes, no event fires (empty changesets are suppressed).
- A handler that throws produces no event (the turn is treated as rolled back).

Events from app handlers carry `source: "handler"`. The same stream also carries `source: "agent"` (chat-assistant writes) and `source: "external"` (the cloud SQL panel). See [UI and changes](/build/ui-and-changes#the-change-stream) for the wire payload and how the UI subscribes.

> Keep `writes` honest anyway — it documents intent and powers chat permissions — but understand it is not what the stream reads.

## Transactions

Wrap multi-statement writes in `db.transaction(...)` for atomicity. The callback is async; the proxy runs an explicit `BEGIN` / `COMMIT` around it on the host connection and `ROLLBACK`s if the callback throws:

```js
export default async ({ body, db }) => {
  const run = db.transaction(async () => {
    await db.prepare('INSERT INTO a (x) VALUES (?)').run(body.x);
    await db.prepare('UPDATE b SET n = n + 1 WHERE id = ?').run(body.id);
  });
  await run();
  return { ok: true };
};
```

Prefer `db.transaction` over issuing raw `BEGIN` / `COMMIT` strings yourself — it owns the rollback path correctly across the worker boundary.

## Timeout

Action handlers are terminated if they run longer than **30 seconds** (longer than queries' 10s, since actions often do more — multi-statement writes, external calls). This is a fixed runtime limit, not an `app.json` setting.

## Multi-step actions

For actions that do several writes or call external services, structure the work as a sequence and wrap risky steps so the function still reaches `return`:

```js
export default async ({ body, db, log, ctx }) => {
  try {
    const res = await ctx.fetch('https://example.com/api', { method: 'POST' });
    if (!res.ok) log.warn(`upstream returned ${res.status}`);
  } catch (err) {
    log.warn(`upstream call failed: ${err.message}`);
  }
  await db.prepare('INSERT INTO events (kind) VALUES (?)').run('synced');
  return { status: 200, body: { ok: true } };
};
```

## Actions are not automation handlers

An action is a tool — a single write invoked through `centraid_write`, receiving `{ body, db, log, app, ctx }`. An **automation** handler is a different file (`automations/<id>/handler.js`) with a different signature — it receives `{ ctx, log }` only (no `db`, no `body`), and its `ctx` is the richer automation surface (`ctx.tool`, `ctx.agent`, `ctx.state`, `ctx.runs`). See the [Automations](/automations/index) tab.
